_meta: GAME_DATA.ratings — the D94 rating system (the OVR / Madden layer) data spine. Design law: RATING-SYSTEM-DESIGN.md. R-0: pure DATA + tables read by src/tactical/T14-ratings.js. NOTHING here is wired into combat yet (byte-identical). Attributes are 0-100, NEUTRAL at 64 (matching commandLeadership's anchor); a missing attribute reads 64 -> no-op. Provenance: 2+ sources = Verified, else Inferred/Disputed (D92 rigor).
_attrNote: 15 attributes (D94 R2). General-tier strategic skills are composites: operationalManeuver=f(tactical,initiative); grandTacticalCoordination=f(command,discipline); plus the standalone logistics + political. aggression is BIDIRECTIONAL and is EXCLUDED from the headline OVR roll-up (D94 anti-Lost-Cause guard) — it bends the Attack tilt instead.
_ovrNote: individual weights sum to 1.0 and DELIBERATELY omit aggression (bidirectional) + the arm sub-skills (those flow into matchups/badges, not the headline). officerSkillSeed (command/tactical/discipline) is the persona->gen.skill projection used for the calibration oracle; it reproduces the authored leader quality via fldClamp((seed-42)/46,0.18,0.95). brigade materiel bumped 0.25->0.28 / condition 0.18->0.15 (D94 §14 rec: armory choices should visibly move OVR).
_dualNote: OVR_attack = headline + round((aggression-64)*0.25 + (initiative-64)*0.10); OVR_defend = headline + round((resolve-64)*0.20 + (grit-64)*0.10). aggression — out of the headline — drives the Attack tilt so recklessness shows up exactly where it should. Plus per-badge attack/defend bias added at R-3.
_gradeNote: A-F report card (Aaron-locked, no gaming S tier). A+ >=90 = the Legendary club. The five lower words+colors are _cmdLeadWord verbatim (one vocabulary). Neutral 64 -> C/Steady.
_genNote: fldDerivePerson generates an unnamed person's attributes = rankBase[rank] + branchTilt[branch] + yearDrift[year] + seededJitter(pid,+/-jitterSpan). Jitter is DETERMINISTIC from a pid hash (no runtime RNG). Generated -> provenance Inferred, pinned near 64; never invents a HIGH number. CALIBRATION ORACLE: this table is regression-locked against the 9 shipped scenarios' authored quality/xp before any mass generation.
_r5Note: D94 R-5 (the prosopography scale-out): the LAZY-MATERIALIZATION sample template a brigade inspect/muster-roll builds rows from. fldBrigadeMuster materializes ONE row per slot here (HARD-capped <=6), NEVER u.men rows; the brigade token carries one recomputable fldMenMeanOVR instead (§8 scale rule). Generated rows are Inferred (a hatched bar via fldProvenanceStyle marks a derived rating distinct from a sourced one, §10). fldPromotePerson is the latent-command play-as-anyone hook; fldPersonTeam the EA-style journey hook.

promotion._note: Q9 (D102): THE GM PROMOTION ECONOMY — the first depth-chart MOVE (RATING-SYSTEM-DESIGN §12.2 / §14.3 / §15). The President promotes a general one grade up the strategic ladder, spending the MULTI-CURRENCY (political capital, primary + a seniority pool) and MERIT-gated by reputation. Promotion raises the general's grade -> a small BOUNDED effective-skill lift (rankBase delta * skillLiftWeight, clamped to skillLiftCap) that reaches the bridge ONLY via the existing _cmdEffectiveSkill -> _cmdGenRating -> commandLeadership pipe — an INPUT, never the scoreboard (honors D74/D94 no-fudge). EARNED (rep >= the grade's meritBar) -> a confidence rep bump; ABOVE-MERIT -> the aboveMeritMul capital surcharge + a jealousy rep hit (so over-promoting a favorite can LOWER his OVR — the honest teaching). LEAPFROGGING a passed-over senior costs extra seniority. BYTE-IDENTICAL until the player promotes (no promotion stored -> _cmdEffectiveSkill returns its pre-Q9 value). DEFERRED to a later GM increment (logged D102): the symmetric AI-GM (needs enemy-commander->combat wiring), cross-theater Transfer (needs authored theaters), Commission + the full corps/division depth-chart tree (needs the strategic<->tactical OOB mapping, the Q8b blocker family).

scout._note: Q8b (D107): THE BETWEEN-BATTLE CAVALRY RECONNAISSANCE — the camp-loop's deferred SCOUTING (RATING-SYSTEM-DESIGN §15 'Scouting = simmable, tiered by recon quality, scales with your cavalry rating'; deferred at D100 until the D106 OOB-mapping substrate existed). The President orders a reconnaissance of the next engagement on the Command desk; it spends `cost` political capital (C.clock.capital, the existing GM currency — no new resource, §12) and reveals the enemy Order of Battle to a tier scaled by the appointed army commander's persona `cavalry` attribute: at/above `cavalryFullThreshold` -> the FULL enemy OOB (corps + brigades, the 'great' recon of §15); below it -> a 'better' recon (the enemy commander named + per-corps grade + posture/terrain advisory, no per-brigade detail). Unscouted -> 'light' (the passive estimate: force grade band + rough brigade count). `baselineCavalry` = the recon quality when no general is appointed (the default historical command). PURE DISPLAY + an economy spend: scouting writes ONLY cmd.scout (the revealed tier, keyed to the next-battle id so intel is fresh per engagement) + debits capital — it NEVER touches combat, so a campaign that never scouts is byte-identical to the pre-Q8b build (the no-fudge wall, build-gate 4d, scans 35-command.js + T15-oob.js). Teaches that good cavalry leadership buys good intelligence — Stuart's reconnaissance rides, and the catastrophic intelligence vacuum at Gettysburg when his cavalry was absent (a real Confederate command failure, not glorified); the Union cavalry's coming-of-age at Brandy Station (1863) and under Sheridan (1864). Inferred designer calibration; the historical narrative is sourced.

corpsCommand._note: Q10 (D108): THE CORPS DEPTH-CHART — the GM depth-chart MOVE of RATING-SYSTEM-DESIGN §12.1/§12.2/§12.4 ('an army version of waiver/trading/transfer'), built on the D106 OOB tree. Beyond appointing the ARMY commander (the existing cmdAppoint billet), the President now SEATS pool generals into the army's CORPS billets (I..IV Corps), spending `seatCost` political capital (C.clock.capital, the existing GM currency — no new resource, §12). HOW IT REACHES THE FIGHT (no new combat path, honors D74/D94 no-fudge): seated corps commanders only shape the army's LEADERSHIP facet — an INPUT the bridge already reads — via a small BOUNDED lift folded into commandLeadership: lift = clamp( sum over seated slots of (effectiveCorpsRating - 64) * perSlotWeight, -liftCap, +liftCap ). effectiveCorpsRating = the general's GM OVR (_cmdGenRating, which already carries the Q9 promotion lift) minus belowGradePenalty when his current grade is below `preferredGrade` (a corps is properly a Maj. Gen.'s billet — seat a junior man and he commands stretched; promote him first to remove the penalty — the Q9 synergy). BYTE-IDENTICAL until the player seats a corps (no corps seated -> sum over zero slots -> lift 0 -> commandLeadership unchanged -> the sandbox + all 9 battles identical). The seated commanders also NAME the player's derived corps on the D106 OOB board (pure display; the predicted edge is unchanged — leadership is captured in the actual fight, never double-counted). A vacant billet contributes 0 (not a baseline penalty — that would break byte-identity); the cost of a weak/leaderless corps emerges as a smaller-or-negative lift relative to a sound staff. Teaches real command organization: armies fought as corps, and WHO led each corps mattered — Lee's First (Longstreet) and Second (Jackson) Corps against the Army of the Potomac's churning corps commands; the rout of Howard's XI Corps at Chancellorsville. Both sides' corps leadership is rated honestly (anti-Lost-Cause). Inferred designer calibration; the corps-organization history is sourced.
corpsCommand._preferredGradeNote: SIDE-AWARE, and historically precise: a UNION corps was a MAJOR GENERAL's command (the US never used a higher field grade for a corps for most of the war); a CONFEDERATE corps was a LIEUTENANT GENERAL's command — the CS authorized the Lt. Gen. grade by Act of Sept. 18, 1862 and promoted Longstreet (First Corps) and Jackson (Second Corps) that October to command the Army of Northern Virginia's new corps (formally organized by General Orders No. 6, Nov. 6, 1862). So a Maj. Gen. seated over a CS corps (e.g. the cavalryman Stuart) leads BELOW grade until promoted to Lt. Gen. — the Q9 synergy bites on the CS roster; on the US roster every named general already meets the Maj. Gen. floor (a correct latent guard for any future junior/commissioned officer).
src: Stephen W. Sears, George B. McClellan: The Young Napoleon (1988)
Joseph T. Glatthaar, General Lee's Army: From Victory to Collapse (2008)

divisionCommand._note: Q12 (D110): THE DIVISION SUB-TIER — the next rung of RATING-SYSTEM-DESIGN §12.1's billet tree (Army -> Corps -> DIVISION -> Brigade), built HIERARCHICALLY on the Q10 corps depth chart (D108). A division could be staffed only UNDER a seated corps (the chain of command: a division belonged to a corps), so the depth chart becomes a real tree the President builds top-down. The President SEATS pool generals into each seated corps's division billets (perCorps divisions), spending `seatCost` political capital (C.clock.capital, the existing GM currency — no new resource, §12). HOW IT REACHES THE FIGHT (no new combat path, honors D74/D94 no-fudge): seated division commanders only shape the army's LEADERSHIP facet — an INPUT the bridge already reads — via a small BOUNDED lift folded into commandLeadership, summed exactly like the corps lift: lift = clamp( sum over seated divisions of (effectiveDivisionRating - 64) * perSlotWeight, -liftCap, +liftCap ). effectiveDivisionRating = the general's GM OVR (_cmdGenRating, carries the Q9 promotion lift) minus belowGradePenalty when his current grade is below `preferredGrade`. The lift is DELIBERATELY SMALLER than the corps lift (liftCap 2 vs the corps' 4, perSlotWeight 0.03 vs 0.05) so the influence hierarchy holds — the army commander weighs most (~±17, §27), then the corps staff (±4), then the divisions (±2): a lower tier is a smaller modifier, never dominant. BYTE-IDENTICAL until the player seats a division (no division seated -> sum over zero slots -> lift 0 -> commandLeadership unchanged -> the sandbox + all 9 battles identical). A vacant division contributes 0 (not a baseline penalty — that would break byte-identity). ONE BILLET PER MAN: a general holds at most one of {army command, a corps, a division}; seating him in a division clears any corps/division he held, and appointing/seating him higher clears his division. Vacating a corps CASCADES — its divisions are orphaned and dropped (cmdInit sanitizes on load). Teaches that a corps was only as good as its divisions: A.P. Hill's hard-hitting 'Light Division' and Hood's and Pickett's divisions were the cutting edge of Lee's corps — as were the Union's hard-fighting divisions under Hancock's II Corps (Gibbon, Barlow) at Gettysburg, the Wilderness, and Spotsylvania — while a corps with weak divisions underdelivered. Both sides' division leadership is rated honestly (anti-Lost-Cause); the teaching exemplars name both armies, never the Confederacy alone (the Q12 bug-hunt LOW, D110 close-out). Inferred designer calibration; the division-organization history is sourced.
divisionCommand._preferredGradeNote: A Civil War DIVISION was properly a MAJOR GENERAL's command in BOTH armies — unlike the corps tier (a US corps a Maj. Gen.'s, a CS corps a Lt. Gen.'s billet). Many divisions were in fact led by Brigadier Generals, especially early in the war (a junior man leads stretched until promoted — the same Q9 synergy as the corps tier, here biting on any Brig. Gen. seated over a division). So preferredGrade is a bare Maj. Gen. for both sides (the map form is kept for symmetry with corpsCommand and to allow a future side-specific refinement).
src: Joseph T. Glatthaar, General Lee's Army: From Victory to Collapse (2008)
Russell F. Weigley, A Great Civil War: A Military and Political History, 1861-1865 (2000)

commission._note: Q11 (D109): THE COMMISSION MOVE — the GM depth-chart move that brings a new officer into the pool (RATING-SYSTEM-DESIGN §12.2/§12.3). The President COMMISSIONS the documented POLITICAL GENERALS (data/generals.json -> sides[side].commissionPool: US Banks/Butler/Sigel/McClernand, CS Floyd/Pillow) for political capital (C.clock.capital, the existing GM currency — no new resource, §12). A political general is CHEAP (costPolitical) but WEAK in the field — Lincoln and Davis took these men for the factions, votes, and immigrant regiments they delivered, not their generalship — so once you appoint or seat him, his LOW OVR drags the army's leadership through the very same _cmdGenRating -> commandLeadership pipe every appointment uses (an INPUT, never the scoreboard; build-gate 4d scans 35-command.js). A proven PROFESSIONAL would cost costProfessional (the field exists for a future expansion; the shipped pool is all political). maxCommissions caps the slate (a scarce budget — you cannot commission everyone). BYTE-IDENTICAL until the player commissions: cmdCommission writes ONLY cmd.commissioned + capital + a reputation seed; with nobody commissioned the appoint/promote/corps pools are exactly the starting roster, so commandLeadership and the 9 battles are identical. DEFERRED + LOGGED (the next GM increment, needs the 1864-election state): the §12.3 ELECTION-SUPPORT bind — a political general delivers faction/election support while he serves, and relieving him before the election costs that support (not just capital); here the relief cost is already taught via his `relief` class (_cmdReliefCost). Inferred designer calibration; the command-politics history is sourced.
src: T. Harry Williams, Lincoln and His Generals (1952)
James M. McPherson, Battle Cry of Freedom (1988)

devTraits._note: D105 (RATING SYSTEM, the LIVE DEV-TRAITS milestone) — the Madden development arc, completing RATING-SYSTEM-DESIGN §9-R-6's last open item ('reputation level-up -> OVR still TODO') + the D94-fork-#5 lock ('Reputation->OVR dev-traits = LIVE in-campaign'). cmdOnResolve already evolves a general's reputation by battle outcome (decisive win +6 / win +3 / draw -1 / loss -4 / decisive loss -8) and _cmdGenRating (0.55*skill+0.45*reputation) -> commandLeadership already reads it, so winning a campaign already nudges his OVR. This block adds the Madden DEV-TRAIT that SHAPES that arc: a hidden potential CEILING (how high he can climb), a FLOOR (how far he can fall), a development RATE (how fast wins/losses move him), and an attritionDrag (the §15-R3 'decline with heavy attrition'). ACCURATE INPUTS, never an output gate (D74/D92): a dev-trait only shapes the general's REPUTATION — an input the existing _cmdGenRating/commandLeadership pipe already consumes — it NEVER writes a casualty/winner. BYTE-IDENTICAL by construction: a general with NO entry in `assign` (or absent devTraits data) takes the literal pre-D105 path (clamp [5,98], rate 1, no drag); the deliberate, citation-grade, historically-directed change is the AUTHORED `assign` map (mirrors how R-6's rosterBadges deliberately changed combat). The ceiling/floor are RELATIVE to the general's SEEDED starting reputation (devTrack.start, fixed) so every assigned general keeps headroom to rise and room to fall (ceilingAbove>=5, floorBelow>=5) — the existing 'a winning general gains reputation / a losing one loses it' invariant holds for all. ANTI-LOST-CAUSE balance: the Union's elite are RISERS (Grant/Sherman/Sheridan/Thomas develop to the summit — the honest thesis that the Union found and elevated its best command) rated AS HIGH as the Confederacy's born legends (Lee/Jackson); the command failures are named plainly on BOTH sides (US McClellan plateau, Burnside/Hooker overpromoted; CS Bragg quarrelsome, Hood volatile-disaster).

tuning._tuningNote: attritionPivot mirrors the camp loop's casualty-SHARE pivot (this side's casualties / both-sides total; the D100 unit-correct measure). A battle this side bled the lion's share applies an extra reputation drag = clamp(attritionDrag * (share - pivot) * attritionDragScale, 0, attritionDragMax) for a trait with attritionDrag>0 (0 by default -> byte-identical). Bounded: at the worst (share 1.0) the drag tops out at attritionDragMax.

rising_star.label: Rising Star
rising_star.desc: Climbs from obscurity to the summit; a near-disaster does not sink him.

steady_rock.label: Steady Rock
steady_rock.desc: Unflappable — rises slow and sure, and almost never breaks.

established_legend.label: Established Legend
established_legend.desc: Already at his peak; holds his name through the bloodiest fields.

natural_genius.label: Natural Genius
natural_genius.desc: Untrained brilliance; rises on raw merit where the schooled stall.

steady_professional.label: Steady Professional
steady_professional.desc: Competent and durable; develops at the ordinary pace.

cautious_fabian.label: Cautious Fabian
cautious_fabian.desc: Preserves the army over the bold stroke; a measured ceiling, but resilient.

mercurial_cavalier.label: Mercurial Cavalier
mercurial_cavalier.desc: Brilliant and beloved, but reputation-sensitive — one lapse bruises the legend.

administrator.label: Administrator
administrator.desc: An organizer's gifts behind a desk; capable, but no field ascent.

plateau.label: Plateau
plateau.desc: An organizer's gifts with a low ceiling — the wins never become the killing blow, and the standing is brittle.

overpromoted.label: Overpromoted
overpromoted.desc: Raised past his level; the great test brings a hard fall.

volatile_aggressor.label: Volatile Aggressor
volatile_aggressor.desc: Dazzling on the attack, but the costly assaults his aggression invites can crater his army and his name.

quarrelsome.label: Quarrelsome
quarrelsome.desc: Capable administration ruined by feud and piecemeal commitment; the gains never stick.

devTraits._archNote: Each archetype: label + glyph (DECORATIVE — the read-out triple-encodes word + polarity sign + number; the glyph is a 4th, font-robust geometric channel) + polarity (+/=/~/−) + the arc tuple. ceilingAbove/floorBelow are headroom RELATIVE to the general's seeded starting reputation (so the absolute ceiling = min(98, start+ceilingAbove), floor = max(5, start-floorBelow)). riseRate/fallRate scale the per-battle reputation delta in the rise/fall direction. attritionDrag scales the extra reputation loss when the army bled the lion's share (the §15-R3 decline-with-attrition). All bounded; every archetype keeps ceilingAbove>=5 & floorBelow>=5 so an assigned general always has room to rise AND fall (preserving the pre-D105 evolution invariant).

src: Brooks D. Simpson, Ulysses S. Grant: Triumph over Adversity (2000)
Ron Chernow, Grant (2017)
us-grant.note: From a failed quartermaster's clerk to General-in-Chief; the near-rout at Shiloh's first day did not end him.

src: John F. Marszalek, Sherman: A Soldier's Passion for Order (1993)
us-sherman.note: From a near-breakdown and a newspaper 'insane' slur to the March to the Sea.

src: Roy Morris Jr., Sheridan: The Life and Wars of General Phil Sheridan (1992)
us-sheridan.note: An infantry quartermaster who rose through cavalry command to the Shenandoah and Five Forks.

src: Christopher J. Einolf, George Thomas: Virginian for the Union (2007)
Larry J. Daniel, Days of Glory: The Army of the Cumberland, 1861-1865 (2004)
us-thomas.note: The Rock of Chickamauga — never broke, never panicked, rose slowly against Grant's and Halleck's distrust.

src: Tom Huntington, Searching for George Gordon Meade (2013)
us-meade.note: The 'Old Snapping Turtle' engineer — won the defensive masterpiece at Gettysburg, then overshadowed.

src: Timothy D. Johnson, Winfield Scott: The Quest for Military Glory (1998)
us-scott.note: The aged author of the Anaconda Plan — strategic vision intact, but past field command.

src: John F. Marszalek, Commander of All Lincoln's Armies: A Life of General Henry W. Halleck (2004)
us-halleck.note: 'Old Brains' — an able administrator and chief of staff who never developed as a field commander.

src: Stephen W. Sears, George B. McClellan: The Young Napoleon (1988)
Ethan S. Rafuse, McClellan's War (2005)
us-mcclellan.note: Built the Army of the Potomac brilliantly; 'the Slows' kept the wins from ever becoming the killing blow.

src: William Marvel, Burnside (1991)
us-burnside.note: A capable corps commander who twice doubted his fitness for army command — and was overwhelmed at Fredericksburg, where he ordered the futile, bloody assaults on the stone wall at Marye's Heights.

src: Walter H. Hebert, Fighting Joe Hooker (1944)
Stephen W. Sears, Chancellorsville (1996)
us-hooker.note: Reorganized the army superbly, rose to its command, then lost his nerve at Chancellorsville.

src: Emory M. Thomas, Robert E. Lee: A Biography (1995)
Michael Fellman, The Making of Robert E. Lee (2000)
cs-lee.note: Entered the war already eminent; held his name through Antietam and Gettysburg alike. (Anti-Lost-Cause sources, not Freeman — see D103.)

src: James I. Robertson Jr., Stonewall Jackson: The Man, the Soldier, the Legend (1997)
cs-jackson.note: The Valley legend — a high floor a single bad day could not erode (until Chancellorsville ended him).

src: Jack Hurst, Nathan Bedford Forrest: A Biography (1993)
Brian Steel Wills, A Battle from the Start: The Life of Nathan Bedford Forrest (1992)
cs-forrest.note: No formal training; 'get there first with the most men' — raw tactical genius that rose on merit. (His postwar Klan leadership is named in the teaching layer, not laundered.)

src: Jeffry D. Wert, General James Longstreet (1993)
William Garrett Piston, Lee's Tarnished Lieutenant (1987)
cs-longstreet.note: The 'Old War Horse' — durable, defensively superb, his reputation later slandered by the Lost Cause for telling the truth about Gettysburg.

src: Charles P. Roland, Albert Sidney Johnston: Soldier of Three Republics (1964)
cs-johnston-a.note: The Confederacy's great early hope — aggressive and capable, killed leading the attack at Shiloh.

src: Craig L. Symonds, Joseph E. Johnston: A Civil War Biography (1992)
cs-johnston-j.note: The Fabian who traded space for the army's survival before Atlanta — measured, resilient, ever at odds with Davis.

src: T. Harry Williams, P.G.T. Beauregard: Napoleon in Gray (1955)
cs-beauregard.note: The hero of Sumter and First Manassas — grand plans and a grand ego, perpetually feuding with Richmond.

src: Emory M. Thomas, Bold Dragoon: The Life of J.E.B. Stuart (1986)
cs-stuart.note: The beau sabreur and eyes of the army — brilliant, beloved, and bruised by the ride that left Lee blind at Gettysburg.

src: Earl J. Hess, Braxton Bragg: The Most Hated Man of the Confederacy (2016)
Judith Lee Hallock, Braxton Bragg and Confederate Defeat (1991)
cs-bragg.note: An able organizer undone by feud and piecemeal commitment — his victories at Chickamauga and elsewhere never translated into lasting standing.

src: Richard M. McMurry, John Bell Hood and the War for Southern Independence (1982)
Wiley Sword, The Confederacy's Last Hurrah: Spring Hill, Franklin, and Nashville (1992)
cs-hood.note: A superb brigade and division attacker promoted past his level — the Tennessee campaign's frontal assaults bled his army to destruction.

_capNote: D94-softcap (Aaron: 'soften the cap for power-fantasy', reconciled): the OUTPUT wall is absolute (no rating read at casualty/winner time, ever); the INPUT caps soften and SCALE WITH the B-5 realism slider. Arcade = generous/dramatic dream-team power-fantasy; Historian = the tight historical wall (cmdBonus 0.72 ~= today's FLDO.CMD_BONUS_CAP band). cmdBonus = the per-unit summed command-aura ceiling; badgeLever = the global per-unit per-lever summed badge delta cap; sev = the per-key sev.* band ceiling; xfactor = the X-Factor 'in the zone' amplification ceiling. Balanced ~= today's shipped numbers so the default is unchanged.

star.label: Star
star.note: common, always-on, small static nudge (resolves to a seed at build)

superstar.label: Superstar
superstar.note: named figures, conditional, larger effect gated by an engine-observable trigger

xfactor.label: X-Factor
xfactor.note: rare, ACTIVATED 'in the zone' moment with a dramatic surge (banner+glow), routed through the capped cmdBonus/sev path

badgeDefs.label: Marksman
badgeDefs.note: Steady aimed fire. (R-0 def only; wired R-3.)

badgeDefs.label: Disciplined
badgeDefs.note: Holds formation under fire.

badgeDefs.label: Hardy Marcher
badgeDefs.note: Endures the march, arrives fresh.

badgeDefs.label: Woods-Fighter
badgeDefs.note: Fights well in broken/wooded ground.

badgeDefs.label: Horseman
badgeDefs.note: Natural cavalryman.

badgeDefs.label: Cannoneer
badgeDefs.note: Well-drilled gun crew.

badgeDefs.label: Blooded
badgeDefs.note: Campaign-hardened veterans.

badgeDefs.label: Stonewall
badgeDefs.note: Stands like a stone wall; steadies the line. (Jackson / the Stonewall Bde.)

badgeDefs.label: Rally the Line
badgeDefs.note: Recovers wavering units in radius (Hancock the Superb).

badgeDefs.label: Flank March
badgeDefs.note: The audacious turning movement (Jackson at Chancellorsville).

badgeDefs.label: Massed Battery
badgeDefs.note: Concentrates the guns (Hunt, Alexander).

badgeDefs.label: Feared
badgeDefs.note: His name dents enemy morale (Forrest). Two modern critical biographies (Wills, Hurst), not the Lost-Cause-era hagiography.

badgeDefs.label: Beloved
badgeDefs.note: His men will not break for him (Lee, Cleburne). One solid cite (Symonds on Cleburne); the obvious Lee source (Freeman) is Lost-Cause-tinged and held out, so this stays Inferred until a second non-hagiographic Lee cite is added.

badgeDefs.label: Pontoon Master
badgeDefs.note: Lays bridges fast (the Engineer Works Corps tie-in).

badgeDefs.label: Foot Cavalry
badgeDefs.note: Jackson's infantry that marched like cavalry. ACTIVATES on redeploy.

badgeDefs.label: Rock of Chickamauga
badgeDefs.note: Thomas holds when all else breaks. ACTIVATES when nearby morale collapses.

badgeDefs.label: Bayonet!
badgeDefs.note: Chamberlain's charge when the cartridges run out. ACTIVATES on empty ammo.

badgeDefs.label: Earned in Blood
badgeDefs.note: USCT at New Market Heights — valor under fire that won 14 of the 16 Medals of Honor awarded to Black soldiers (Army) in the war.

badgeDefs.label: The Grand Charge
badgeDefs.note: Pickett's Charge: a huge surge that historically could ALSO break the unit (double-edged). SHIPPED REALITY (R-6 bug-hunt, D104): the engine delivers ONLY the bounded UPSIDE — polarity 'dual' is treated as positive in fldXFactorStep, so this is a capped cmdBonus surge with NO downside lever yet (the `surge_risk`/`mag` fields are not yet consumed; the bounded morale-cost INPUT lever for dual X-Factors is DEFERRED to a future increment). Acceptable because R-6 assigns grand_charge ONLY to cs_johnson_gap (Chickamauga — the Brotherton-gap breakthrough that SUCCEEDED), where an upside-only surge is historically apt, and DECLINES it on Pickett's repulsed charge (the flip-risk case the downside would be needed for). Verified by the standard Pickett's Charge literature (catalog-wide sourcing sweep, D103).

badgeDefs.label: First with the Most
badgeDefs.note: Forrest's maxim — get there first with the most men. ACTIVATES when his cavalry charges a flank; the surge routes through the capped cmdBonus (fldLever none), never a static lever.

badgeDefs.label: Green Levies
badgeDefs.note: Raw regiments, brittle under the first volley. Damper only.

badgeDefs.label: Powder-Shy
badgeDefs.note: Surprised/conscript troops start shaky.

badgeDefs.label: Piecemeal Commitment
badgeDefs.note: Bragg fed brigades in one at a time. A DRAG on his own coordination, never an enemy buff.

badgeDefs.label: The Slows
badgeDefs.note: McClellan's chronic caution — Lincoln's word. Slows his own commitment. No enemy buff.

badgeDefs.label: Rigid Plan
badgeDefs.note: Burnside ground forward to plan against fortified ground (Fredericksburg). Suppresses replan.

badgeDefs.label: Reckless Valor
badgeDefs.note: Hood's all-out aggression — can help the attack BUT raises his own leader-fall risk + exhausts the command (double-edged).

_rosterNote: R-6 (D104): the documented commander/unit traits assigned to the 9 shipped battles' brigade tokens, keyed by scenario id (the fldScenarioRegistry key) -> unit id. fldScenarioRosterBadges() reads this; fldBrSpec stamps it onto the unit (an inline d.badges on a Custom Battle wins). These are ACCURATE INPUTS that nudge the universal D74 levers (rally/fire/speed/melee, hard-capped per fldRatingRealismCap) — NEVER an output gate (D92/D74): a trait pushes the probable result TOWARD its historical direction because the trait caused the history, never by writing a casualty/winner. Sourced + balanced via a 15-agent citation+critic workflow (anti-Lost-Cause: the Union's command flaws — McClellan's the_slows, Burnside's rigid_plan, McClernand/Sherman piecemeal, the green/surprised troops — are named as plainly as the Confederacy's virtues; the Confederacy's flaws — Bragg/Magruder/D.H. Hill piecemeal, green Western corps — are named too). Net (after the D104 bug-hunt trims): CS 12 clear virtues : 6 flaws : 4 double-edged (3 reckless_valor + grand_charge — command flaws / doomed charges that can ALSO help); US 11 virtues : 10 flaws. (CS won 6 of these 9.) BALANCE/HONESTY (D104 bug-hunt): the dual badges are NOT folded into the CS virtue column. TRIGGER FIDELITY: the NON-xfactor situational badges (stonewall/green_levies/powder_shy/rigid_plan/piecemeal/bayonet/rock via the R-3 static fldBadgeFactor) currently apply their SMALL CAPPED static factor ALWAYS-ON for the whole battle — _fldBadgeTrig defaults unrecognized triggers to true; per-situation gating (defend-objective / first-fire / surprised / attack-fortified) is a future R-7 refinement. The magnitudes are tiny + per-lever-capped, and the net effect is validated HISTORICALLY FAITHFUL by the A/B sweep + all 9 per-battle probes passing with badges ON. NOTE: badges with fldLever 'none' (massed_battery, flank_march, reckless_valor) are DISPLAY/teaching only unless they are X-Factors (grand_charge surges the command aura when its assault triggers; the dual downside is NOT yet modeled — see its badgeDef note). the_slows: data/antietam.json has NO McClellan army token (only corps/division), so per D92 it is NOT forced onto every division — it sits on us_sturgis (the P2 Burnside's-Bridge assault, the battle's iconic delayed/hesitant Union attack) as the brigade-level manifestation of the army's slow sequential commitment. grand_charge sits on cs_johnson_gap (Chickamauga — the breakthrough that SUCCEEDED) and was deliberately DECLINED on Pickett's repulsed charge (flip-risk). WINNER FLIPS: the deterministic A/B sweep (sandbox + 9 battles x 3 seeds) shows the badges shift CASUALTIES but essentially never the VERDICT — every phased battle's per-phase winner is byte-identical off->on; the only single-phase winner flip observed is TOWARD the historical winner (e.g. Chancellorsville seed 1 US-timeout -> CS-hold, Jackson's flank march = Lee's victory). There is NO flip AGAINST history. cs_colston_div was dropped (D104 bug-hunt: stonewall is a defend-objective trait, Colston's division was ATTACKING in the flank march; the teaching rides cs_rodes_div's foot_cavalry+flank_march). SHILOH (decide-&-log, the A/B sweep): the headless single-phase Shiloh baseline is already a ~tossup (US ~3/5, badges off) — its day-1-surprise / day-2-recovery arc cannot resolve in one phase. So the Union's caught-napping flaw is carried by the SCENARIO DESIGN (the CS dawn surprise attack) + the teaching cards, NOT a rally penalty on the green Union defenders, which (D92) would flip the single-phase fight AGAINST the historical Union victory. The decisive documented factor — the Hornet's Nest STAND (Prentiss + W.H.L. Wallace, who died holding the line) — is modeled (rock_of_chickamauga + stonewall), nudging the US to ~4/5; the CS disorganization (green/piecemeal) is the honest CS flaw. Accurate inputs, not an output gate.

personas._note: R-0 authors the Bull Run leadership cast (Verified). command/tactical/discipline are CALIBRATED so the persona->quality projection reproduces each leader's AUTHORED quality in data/bullrun.json within tolerance (the calibration oracle). Other attributes are historical character. The full prosopography (every identifiable individual) is the D93 target, built via per-army research workflows over this spine.

ld_mcdowell.name: Irvin McDowell
ld_mcdowell.bio: Planned a sound turning movement at Bull Run, but commanded the rawest army yet fielded; the plan outran the men.

ld_heintzelman.name: Samuel P. Heintzelman
ld_heintzelman.bio: A regular-army division commander; his rank at Bull Run is itself disputed (the D92-surfaced Col. vs Brig. Gen. case).

ld_beauregard.name: P. G. T. Beauregard
ld_beauregard.bio: The hero of Sumter; an engineer by training, fond of Napoleonic grand designs his green army couldn't always execute.

ld_johnston.name: Joseph E. Johnston
ld_johnston.bio: The ranking officer on the field; brought the Shenandoah army by rail — the pivot that won the day.

ld_bee.name: Barnard E. Bee
ld_bee.bio: Rallied his brigade on Henry Hill and — by tradition — named Jackson 'Stonewall' before falling mortally wounded.

ld_bartow.name: Francis S. Bartow
ld_bartow.bio: A Georgia congressman-turned-colonel; killed leading his Georgians against the Union batteries — among the highest-ranking to die that day.

ld_jackson.name: Thomas J. Jackson
ld_jackson.bio: A VMI artillery professor who stood like a stone wall on Henry Hill and earned the name that made him legend. His OVR rises with the war (dev-traits).

generalPersonas._note: R-1 (D94): the STRATEGIC army-commander cast — keyed by the data/generals.json id — so the President's-Desk command pipe is persona-SOURCED. CALIBRATION ORACLE (strategic leg): command/tactical/discipline are set so round(0.45*command + 0.35*tactical + 0.20*discipline) === the Verified gen.skill EXACTLY (fldOfficerSkillSeed -> _cmdEffectiveSkill -> _cmdGenRating), so the appoint-pool words MATCH HISTORY and the whole strategic layer stays byte-identical while the persona becomes the source of truth. The other 12 attributes are Inferred designer calibration anchored to each general's Verified documented character (provenance + sources mirror generals.json; aggression tracks the documented temperament; it is EXCLUDED from the headline OVR roll-up). These are a SEPARATE record from the Bull Run FIELD cast above (war-long army commander vs the 1861 brigade officer); R-5 unifies them into one prosopography person. The 9 = the principal commanders of the 9 D92-hardened battles (Bull Run is covered by the field cast above). NOTE: skill (the calibration target) and the documented gen.skill are Verified; the attribute VECTOR is Inferred calibration.

cs-lee.name: Robert E. Lee
cs-lee.bio: The Army of Northern Virginia's commander — audacious maneuver against long odds (Antietam, Fredericksburg, Chancellorsville, Gettysburg). Anti-Lost-Cause: a brilliant operational improviser whose offensives also bled an irreplaceable army.

cs-jackson.name: Thomas J. Jackson
cs-jackson.bio: Lee's right arm — the Valley campaign and the Chancellorsville flank march, the model of operational speed and audacity. The war-long corps commander (distinct from the 1861 brigadier in the field cast above).

cs-johnston-a.name: Albert Sidney Johnston
cs-johnston-a.bio: Davis's favorite, who held an overextended Western front and led from the front at Shiloh — where he bled to death from a leg wound (the low fate). Surprise won the morning; his death and a piecemeal afternoon lost it.

cs-bragg.name: Braxton Bragg
cs-bragg.bio: A harsh disciplinarian and able organizer who could not coordinate a battle: at Chickamauga he fed brigades in piecemeal and failed to pursue a won field. The Verified, plainly-named flaw (anti-Lost-Cause: flaws rate as honestly as virtues).

cs-longstreet.name: James Longstreet
cs-longstreet.bio: Lee's 'Old War Horse' — a modern defensive tactician whose Chickamauga breakthrough decided the day. Anti-Lost-Cause: scapegoated postwar precisely because his clear-eyed defensive doctrine and postwar Republicanism affronted the Lost Cause myth.

us-mcclellan.name: George B. McClellan
us-mcclellan.bio: The 'Young Napoleon' — a superb organizer beloved by his men, but chronically over-cautious (Lincoln's 'the slows'): he built the Army of the Potomac and then would not risk it (Malvern Hill, Antietam). His genius is in logistics/organization, not the attack.

us-grant.name: Ulysses S. Grant
us-grant.bio: The general who won the war — relentless operational pressure, calm under fire, total-war strategy (Donelson, Shiloh, Vicksburg, the Overland Campaign). Anti-Lost-Cause: the 'butcher' label is a Lost Cause distortion; his loss rates were comparable to Lee's against a smaller foe.

us-burnside.name: Ambrose E. Burnside
us-burnside.bio: Genial, earnest, and honest about being overmatched in army command — he ground his army forward to a fixed plan against the stone wall at Fredericksburg. The Verified flaw is the rigidity, not the man's character.

us-meade.name: George G. Meade
us-meade.bio: The irascible 'Old Snapping Turtle' and engineer — a steady, defensive-minded professional who won the defensive masterpiece at Gettysburg, then drew Lincoln's frustration for a cautious pursuit.

generalPersonas._commissionPersonaNote: Q11 (D109): the COMMISSION-POOL personas (keyed to data/generals.json sides[side].commissionPool ids). Same persona shape as the army-commander cast above, but for the POLITICAL GENERALS — command/tactical are LOW (the documented weakness) while `political` is HIGH (the constituency they delivered). command/tactical/discipline are calibrated so round(fldOfficerSkillSeed) === the entry's skill EXACTLY (the same oracle the strategic cast uses), so _cmdEffectiveSkill -> _cmdGenRating yields a LOW OVR and seating/appointing one teaches that rank and constituency are not competence. prov 'Inferred' — the rating VECTOR is a calibrated reading of a documented career; the biography is sourced (>=2 real works) in the commissionPool entry.

us-banks.name: Nathaniel P. Banks
sources: James G. Hollandsworth Jr., Pretense of Glory: The Life of General Nathaniel P. Banks (1998)
Gary D. Joiner, One Damn Blunder from Beginning to End: The Red River Campaign of 1864 (2003)
us-banks.bio: The 'Bobbin Boy' — ex-Speaker and governor commissioned for the antislavery votes he brought; a poor field commander (the Valley, Port Hudson, Red River) who nonetheless built Black regiments and steered Louisiana toward emancipation. A POLITICAL GENERAL (commission pool).

us-butler.name: Benjamin F. Butler
sources: Elizabeth D. Leonard, Benjamin Franklin Butler: A Noisy, Fearless Life (2022)
Chester G. Hearn, When the Devil Came Down to Dixie: Ben Butler in New Orleans (1997)
us-butler.bio: 'Beast' Butler — the War Democrat the Union could not afford to fire and could not trust with a battle; his lasting mark was the 1861 'contraband' decision, not generalship. A POLITICAL GENERAL (commission pool).

us-sigel.name: Franz Sigel
sources: Stephen D. Engle, Yankee Dutchman: The Life of Franz Sigel (1993)
William Shea and Earl Hess, Pea Ridge: Civil War Campaign in the West (1992)
us-sigel.bio: The 'Yankee Dutchman' — kept for the German-American vote ('I fights mit Sigel'); a trained gunner out of his depth at army command, beaten at Wilson's Creek and routed at New Market. A POLITICAL GENERAL (commission pool).

us-mcclernand.name: John A. McClernand
sources: Richard L. Kiper, Major General John Alexander McClernand: Politician in Uniform (1999)
Edwin C. Bearss, The Campaign for Vicksburg (1985-86)
us-mcclernand.bio: Lincoln's fellow Illinoisan and a War Democrat kept for his coalition value — brave under fire but chronically insubordinate and self-promoting; relieved by Grant at Vicksburg. A POLITICAL GENERAL (commission pool).

cs-floyd.name: John B. Floyd
sources: Benjamin Franklin Cooling, Forts Henry and Donelson: The Key to the Confederate Heartland (1987)
Ezra J. Warner, Generals in Gray: Lives of the Confederate Commanders (1959)
cs-floyd.bio: Ex-Governor of Virginia and U.S. War Secretary, commissioned for political weight; lost the western Virginia campaign, then fled Fort Donelson before its surrender — conduct Davis found disgraceful. A POLITICAL GENERAL (commission pool).

cs-pillow.name: Gideon J. Pillow
sources: Nathaniel Cheairs Hughes Jr. and Roy P. Stonesifer Jr., The Life and Wars of Gideon J. Pillow (1993)
Benjamin Franklin Cooling, Forts Henry and Donelson: The Key to the Confederate Heartland (1987)
cs-pillow.bio: A Polk law-partner whose every star came from politics; at Fort Donelson he squandered a won breakout and fled the surrender he helped make inevitable. His CSA rank was brigadier general (the 'major general' was a Mexican War / state-militia title). A POLITICAL GENERAL (commission pool).
